did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically transformer architectures (DeiT-B, ViT-B, Swin Transformer, DaViT, ConvNeXt), to perform automated skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dermoscopic dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from fine-tuning five transformer architectures (DeiT-B, ViT-B, Swin Transformer, DaViT, ConvNeXt) on the HAM10000 dataset for skin lesion classification. It includes specific methodology, training details, and quantitative performance metrics (e.g., 90.60% accuracy for DeiT-B), which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Data-Efficient Image Transformers for Skin Lesion Classification
journal: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
brief_summary: This study evaluates the efficacy of five advanced transformer architectures—DeiT-B, ViT-B, Swin Transformer, DaViT, and ConvNeXt—for automated skin lesion classification using the imbalanced HAM10000 dermoscopic dataset. All models were fine-tuned from ImageNet-1K pretrained weights and assessed on a test partition comprising 20% of the data. The DeiT-B architecture achieved the highest performance with 90.60% classification accuracy and an 82.94% macro-averaged F1-score, outperforming the other evaluated models. The results indicate that efficiency-focused transformer designs effectively address class imbalance challenges inherent in dermoscopic datasets. Consequently, the study concludes that transformers offer superior generalization and resilience compared to traditional CNNs for this diagnostic task.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. 327 total images (228 training, 34 validation, 65 testing).
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. The paper notes 'bce (90)' correctly classified in the confusion matrix discussion, though Table 1 lists the class as 'bee' with 514 images.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. 1099 total images. The paper notes 'bkl (184)' correctly classified in the confusion matrix discussion.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. 115 total images. Identified as an underrepresented class with limited samples.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. 1113 total images. The paper notes '153 mel (melanoma) cases correctly identified' in the confusion matrix discussion.
The predominant class in the HAM10000 dataset with 6705 total images. The paper notes '1304 nv (nevus) samples accurately classified' in the confusion matrix discussion.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. 142 total images. Identified as an underrepresented class with limited samples.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin lesion diagnostic categories (akiec, bee, bkl, df, mel, nv, vase)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of dermoscopic images into seven discrete diagnostic categories using the HAM10000 dataset. The primary metric reported is classification accuracy (90.60% for DeiT-B) and macro-averaged F1-score (82.94%).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images obtained from a Kaggle library, containing 10,015 RGB images (600x450 pixels) organized into seven diagnostic categories. The dataset is characterized by significant class imbalance.

use.training: 70% of the dataset was used for training the transformer models (DeiT-B, ViT-B, Swin Transformer, DaViT, ConvNeXt).
use.validation: 10% of the dataset was used for validation and hyperparameter fine-tuning.
use.testing: 20% of the dataset was used for testing and performance evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To guarantee reproducibility and enforce deterministic computation during model training.
Used as a regularization technique during training to prevent overconfidence and improve generalization.
Reported alongside precision, recall, and F1-score metrics in Table III to indicate variability or uncertainty in the per-class performance estimates.

statistical_methods_used.method: Comparative analysis of mean performance metrics
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the classification accuracy and macro-averaged F1-score of the DeiT-B model against other transformer architectures (ViT, Swin, ConvNeXt, DaViT) and prior CNN-based studies.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive statistics (accuracy and F1-scores) and confidence intervals (± values) reported in Table III, but no statistical significance testing (e.g., t-test, ANOVA) was performed or reported.

HAM10000.notes: The DeiT-B model achieved the highest accuracy among the evaluated transformers. The study notes that no balancing techniques were utilized, and the dataset is imbalanced. Misclassifications frequently occurred between clinically analogous categories, such as melanoma being predicted as nevi or benign keratosis lesions misidentified as nevi. The confusion matrix showed diagonal dominance. Training loss declined from 1.12 to 0.56 over 30 epochs, while validation loss stabilized around 0.70. Training accuracy reached ~95.5% and validation accuracy leveled off around 89-90%.

backbone_components: Linear projection of non-overlapping 16x16 patches into patch tokens
Learnable classification token (CLS) at the start of the sequence
Learnable distillation token (DST) at the end of the sequence

encoder_block_details.attention_mechanism: Multi-Head Self-Attention (MSA) with scaled dot-product attention

architecture_details.distillation_mechanism: Knowledge distillation from a CNN-based teacher model (e.g., ResNet) using label smoothing cross-entropy loss combined with distillation loss

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to model-specific input dimensions (e.g., 224x224 pixels for DeiT-B and ViT-B; 256x256 pixels for Swin-B)
Validation and testing phase resizing to 105% of the model's input size
Center-cropping resized images to match precise input dimensions
Channel-wise normalization using ImageNet statistics: subtracting mean values (R: 0.485, G: 0.456, B: 0.406) and dividing by standard deviations (R: 0.229, G: 0.224, B: 0.225)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper explicitly states that no balancing techniques or image augmentation methods were utilized during training. The study was conducted on the imbalanced HAM10000 dataset without data replication or augmentation.

conclusions: The DeiT-B (Data-Efficient Image Transformer Base) architecture achieved the highest classification accuracy of 90.60% and a macro-averaged F1-score of 82.94% on the imbalanced HAM10000 dataset, outperforming ViT-B (89.60%), Swin Transformer (89.45%), ConvNeXt (88.65%), and DaViT (87.90%).

Efficiency-focused transformer designs effectively address class imbalance challenges inherent in dermoscopic datasets without requiring data balancing techniques or augmentation, demonstrating superior generalization compared to traditional CNN-based methods.

The proposed DeiT-B model significantly surpassed the performance of prior CNN-based studies (e.g., MobileNet, VGG-16, Xception, DenseNet, AlexNet, RegNetY, Modified Resnet-50) which reported accuracies ranging from 80.61% to 85.80% on similar datasets.

Transformer architectures demonstrate enhanced capability in capturing detailed feature representations from dermoscopic images, offering improved recognition capabilities for underrepresented lesion types compared to CNNs.

Misclassifications frequently occurred between clinically analogous categories, specifically between melanoma and nevi, and between benign keratosis lesions and nevi, highlighting the difficulty of distinguishing visually similar lesions.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the use of an imbalanced dataset without balancing techniques, noting that while DeiT-B performed well, the results are specific to this experimental setup and dataset.
important_remarks: The study was conducted on the imbalanced HAM10000 dataset without utilizing any data balancing techniques or image augmentation methods, which limits the generalizability of the results to balanced clinical scenarios.
No cross-validation was performed; the dataset was partitioned into fixed training (70%), validation (10%), and testing (20%) sets, which may introduce bias dependent on the specific split.

The dataset is heavily skewed towards the 'nv' (melanocytic nevi) class (6,705 images), while underrepresented classes like 'df' (dermatofibroma, 115 images) and 'vase' (vascular lesions, 142 images) pose significant challenges for model learning.

Misclassifications frequently occurred between clinically analogous categories, specifically between melanoma and nevi, and between benign keratosis lesions and nevi, highlighting the difficulty of distinguishing visually similar lesions.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets or multi-center cohorts, relying solely on the internal test partition of the HAM10000 dataset.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, t-tests) was reported to confirm the statistical significance of the performance differences between the transformer architectures.
The models were fine-tuned from ImageNet-1K pretrained weights, which may introduce a domain gap given that ImageNet contains natural images rather than medical dermoscopic images.

The hardware used for training was a single NVIDIA Tesla P100 GPU, and the study was conducted in Kaggle Notebooks, which may limit reproducibility regarding computational resources and environment specifics.
